problem_summary: 2010 MCM Problem B (Geographic Profiling / "Crime Busting"): given the locations and times of a serial criminal's past offenses, build a model to predict the criminal's anchor/home location and the location and time of the next crime, and present the output usefully to police. This team (Control #8362) treats it as a discrete grid/probability-mapping problem rooted in criminology's "distance decay" and "circle hypothesis" theories, producing color-gradient probability heatmaps that give police a prioritized search order. Validated against five real serial-killer datasets (Sutcliffe, Chester Turner, Zodiac, Jack the Ripper, Grim Sleeper) geocoded from Google Earth/CommunityWalk.
models_used: Grid discretization of the search area: the candidate region is partitioned into an n×m mesh and a probability is computed at every grid point (a discrete approximation to a continuous probability field), with bilinear interpolation used to smooth the grid for display.

Distance-decay 'gravitational pull' aggregation model for criminal location: each grid point's score = sum over all crime scenes of f(distance(grid point, crime scene)); normalized to a probability distribution. Conceptually a non-normalized kernel-density / superposition-of-kernels estimate.

FIVE explicit distance-decay kernel functions compared head-to-head: (1) Linear truncated f=a−bx; (2) Normal/Gaussian with buffer-zone offset μ (Brantingham & Brantingham 1981); (3) Negative Exponential e^(−cx); (4) Truncated Negative Exponential (linear rise to x0 then exp decay); (5) Plateaued Negative Exponential (flat to x0 then exp decay).

Next-crime-location model via 'minimum perturbation' / least-surprise criterion: a virtual crime is inserted at each grid point, the criminal-location distribution is recomputed, and the sum of squared differences (SSD) versus the original distribution is the deviation score; lowest deviation = most likely next-crime site. Yields a full ΔProb distribution, not just a point.
Linear regression / linear interpolation on inter-crime intervals to predict the TIME of the next crime: t_N = t_{N−1} + (t_{N−1} − t_1)/(N−1) (average inter-event gap extrapolated).

Time-coupling weighting model: a recency weight w(t) = ((d−c)a)/(√t + a) + c multiplies each crime's decay contribution, so recent crimes count more and the model adapts to a criminal's spatial drift over time.
Classical spatial-statistics baselines for intuition/comparison: Mean Center (center of mass), Center of Minimum Distance, Standard Deviational Circle (SDC), Standard Deviational Ellipse (SDE).

Two evaluation metrics framed as a model in themselves: Error Distance (great-circle distance from predicted to true point) and Search Cost (fraction of grid cells searched, in descending-probability order, before hitting the true cell).
key_techniques: Grounding the model in domain literature first: distance decay, the circle hypothesis (87% of serial rapists live inside the circle of the two farthest crimes), and the buffer-zone concept are cited from criminology papers (Santtila, Rhodes & Conly, Brantingham & Brantingham, Snook) and then operationalized as the f(x) kernels — math is motivated, not arbitrary.

Superposition of per-crime kernels into a single additive scalar field — an elegant way to turn a point set into a smooth, normalizable probability map that naturally handles clusters (two clusters auto-produce two hotspots with magnitudes proportional to cluster size).

Reframing 'predict next crime' as 'which new point perturbs the existing distribution least' (SSD between distributions) — a clever self-consistency / least-surprise trick that reuses the same machinery as the location model.

Function abstraction as a design principle: the decay function f and weight function w are passed as inputs, so police can swap kernels per criminal type (burglar vs. serial killer) without changing the algorithm — a single framework, many submodels.

Honest complexity analysis with optimization: location model is O(n²·l)≈O(n²); next-crime model is O(n⁴); they then recover a factor-of-N speedup via caching (reuse the N-point distribution and add only the new point's contribution while preserving normalization) and note near-perfect parallelizability.

Edge-effect correction: a 30–40% boundary buffer zone where probability is computed but no virtual crimes are inserted, removing the artifact that edge points look spuriously good (fewer neighbors → smaller perturbation).

Validation on real, geocoded serial-killer case data with two quantitative metrics and a results table comparing all 5 kernels × coupled/uncoupled — concrete numbers (e.g., Sutcliffe search area cut to ~15%; next-crime error 0.27 km with Neg. Exp.).

Triangulating the prediction quality using simple synthetic cases first (uniform, outline, linear, clustered point spreads) to build intuition about what 'correct' output should look like before running real data.

Controlled experiment for time-coupling: Poisson-process-generated times assigned in 'correlated' vs 'uncorrelated' ways to a near-linear point set, isolating exactly when recency weighting helps (only when space and time correlate).
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins on four axes simultaneously. (1) Domain immersion: it opens with real criminology — distance decay, circle hypothesis, buffer zone, Hickey's Trauma Control Model — and converts each into a mathematical object, so every modeling choice is justified rather than asserted. (2) A genuinely original core idea: the 'gravitational superposition of decay kernels' map plus the 'least-perturbation / minimum-SSD' next-crime predictor is creative and non-obvious, yet simple enough to state in pseudocode. (3) Generality and engineering maturity: abstracting the decay and weight functions into pluggable inputs, plus rigorous big-O analysis with an N× caching speedup and parallelizability, signals a team that thinks like both modelers and engineers. (4) Rigorous, self-critical validation: five REAL serial killers, two well-chosen metrics (error distance and search-cost), a full comparison table, and — crucially — the courage to report that time-coupling sometimes HURT performance and that Chester Turner's home was un-findable because all his crimes were on one side. Judges reward teams that test where their model breaks and say so. The actionable police-facing framing (red=search first, prioritized search order, an Executive Summary letter to the Chief of Police) shows the model answers the actual question asked, not just the math."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-strong and clearly contributed to the win. It leads with a one-page Summary Statement (plain-language: what we built, that Sutcliffe's search area shrank to 15%) AND a separate role-played Executive Summary written as a letter to the Chief of Police that frankly lists strengths AND limitations (commuter criminals, single-base assumption, homogeneous terrain) — judges love this honesty up front. A detailed table of contents signals organization. The body follows clean modeling logic: Background → Problem → Assumptions → Metrics/Functions (building blocks: spatial stats, then the 5 decay kernels with equations and a comparison decay plot) → Our Approach (location, then next-crime, then time, then time-coupling, each with Method / Pseudocode / Why-it-Works / Complexity subsections) → Results (metrics defined, procedure that holds out the most recent crime as a test, comparison table) → Conclusion → Future Research → Appendix with raw geocoded case data and references. Recurring strengths: every algorithm has explicit pseudocode (Programs 1–3), every model has a 'why it works' justification, complexity is stated, and the held-out-last-crime test design is described before numbers are shown. Visuals are heavily used (color-gradient probability heatmaps, decay-function plots, simple-case diagrams) and tied to the red=high-priority police narrative. Real references (11 cited criminology sources) lend credibility."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Single-anchor / non-commuter assumption is baked in: the model assumes one home base and that the criminal lives near the crimes (distance decay). It admits it fails for 'commuter' criminals and for one-sided offenders — Chester Turner, who killed entirely east of his home, was un-locatable (error distance ~58–66 units). The model cannot find a hub that is geometrically removed from the crime cloud.

Homogeneous-space assumption: ignores population density, terrain, road networks, and opportunity, so it can place the predicted base in an uninhabitable area or predict crimes where none are possible. They acknowledge real tools (CrimeStat) incorporate these.

Time-coupling generally DECREASED accuracy in their own tests, which they could only explain as 'bad luck' — a feature presented as an enhancement actually hurt, and they flag it must be resolved before real use. It only helps when space and time are correlated.

Only ONE weighting function w(t) was studied (due to time constraints), so the time model is under-explored and the chosen w is somewhat ad hoc / unmotivated compared to the literature-grounded decay kernels.

Kernel parameters (a, b, c, x0, σ, μ) are not systematically fit or cross-validated to data — they are largely hand-set, so the head-to-head kernel comparison may reflect tuning luck as much as kernel form.
Next-crime predictor is O(n⁴); even with the N× caching speedup it is the scalability bottleneck and limits grid resolution without a cluster.

Validation is small (effectively two killers, Sutcliffe and Turner, drive the results table; the other three datasets appear mainly for the time-prediction table), and the 'search cost' headline (15%) is essentially a single best-case anecdote.

Probabilities are heuristic relative weights (normalized decay sums), not calibrated probabilities — 'probability' is used loosely; there is no statistical model of where the offender actually is, so confidence cannot be quantified.
reusable_patterns: Point-set → scalar field via superposition of decay/influence kernels, then normalize → a reusable recipe any time you must turn scattered events into a priority/probability map (facility siting, outbreak source-finding, demand heatmaps).

Abstract the core function as a pluggable input: parameterize over the kernel f and weight w so one algorithm yields many 'submodels' and you can A/B compare functional forms in a single results table — a strong generality move judges reward.

'Least-perturbation / minimum-SSD' predictor: to predict the next item that fits an existing pattern, insert a candidate, recompute the distribution, and score by how little it disturbs the original — reusable for any 'most consistent next point' prediction.
Hold-out-the-most-recent-event validation: drop the last data point, predict it, measure error — a clean, honest test design that mirrors the real operational situation.

Pair two complementary metrics — a physical error (distance) and a decision-cost metric (search cost = fraction of cells inspected in priority order) — so you measure both accuracy and practical usefulness.
Edge/boundary buffer-zone correction for grid models: compute the field on a padded grid but only evaluate/insert candidates in the interior to kill edge artifacts.

Caching to cut recomputation: when adding one element to an N-element aggregate, reuse the N-element result and add only the new element's contribution (preserving normalization) for an O(N) speedup — applicable to any incremental kernel-sum recomputation.
Build intuition on synthetic canonical cases (uniform, linear, clustered) BEFORE real data, so you can sanity-check that the model produces qualitatively correct output.
Front-load a plain-language Summary + a role-played stakeholder Executive Summary that openly states limitations — establishes credibility and answers 'so what for the client' immediately.
Ground every equation in cited domain literature first, then operationalize the theory into math — makes assumptions defensible.
Report negative results (time-coupling hurt) and known failure modes (one-sided offender) explicitly — self-critical honesty is a hallmark of Outstanding papers, not a weakness.
